description:
You are a support engineer who must quickly gather a short “first-look” diagnostic bundle from a customer system.

Goal
1. Create a directory called /home/user/diagnostics .
2. Inside that directory, create a plain-text file named summary.log that contains **exactly three lines** in the order shown below (no extra blank lines, no leading/trailing spaces):

   HOSTNAME=<host_value>
   KERNEL=<kernel_value>
   DISKROOT=<percentage_used>

   •  <host_value>  – output of the command `hostname`  
   •  <kernel_value> – output of the command `uname -r`  
   •  <percentage_used> – the numeric percentage (without the “%” sign) that appears in the “Use%” column for the root filesystem “/” when the command `df -P /` is executed.  
     ─ Example: if the df line shows “45%”, this field must contain **45** (not **45%**).

3. After the summary.log file is in place, create a gzip-compressed tar archive named diagnostics_bundle.tar.gz that contains **only** the summary.log file and is located directly under /home/user/diagnostics/ .

Verification details (strictly enforced by automated tests)
• /home/user/diagnostics must exist and be a directory.  
• /home/user/diagnostics/summary.log must exist, be readable by the current user, and have exactly three non-empty lines.  
• /home/user/diagnostics/summary.log line 1 must start with “HOSTNAME=” and the value after the equals sign must be byte-for-byte identical to the output of the command `hostname` executed in the same shell.  
• /home/user/diagnostics/summary.log line 2 must start with “KERNEL=” and the value after the equals sign must be identical to the output of `uname -r`.  
• /home/user/diagnostics/summary.log line 3 must start with “DISKROOT=” and the value after the equals sign must be identical to the numeric characters of the “Use%” column for “/” gathered with `df -P / | tail -1`.  The percent sign must **not** appear in the log.  
• /home/user/diagnostics/diagnostics_bundle.tar.gz must exist, be a valid gzip-compressed tar archive, and must contain exactly one member entry named summary.log (no parent directory inside the archive).  
• The summary.log file that resides inside the archive must be byte-for-byte identical to the on-disk /home/user/diagnostics/summary.log file.

The entire job can be accomplished with two or three shell commands; choose whichever sequence you prefer. Make sure relative and absolute paths are used correctly so the archive ends up in /home/user/diagnostics and not elsewhere.

No additional files, sub-directories, or archive members should be produced.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful completion:

1. Directory:
   • Path: /home/user/diagnostics
   • Permissions: default user umask (test runner only checks for existence and that it is a directory).

2. File on disk:
   • Path: /home/user/diagnostics/summary.log
   • Format (exactly three lines, each terminated by “\n”):
        HOSTNAME=$(hostname output at test-time)
        KERNEL=$(uname -r output at test-time)
        DISKROOT=$(df -P / | tail -1 | awk '{print $5}' | tr -d '%')
   • The automated test will regenerate the above three command substitutions at run-time and compare.

3. Archive:
   • Path: /home/user/diagnostics/diagnostics_bundle.tar.gz
   • Must be a valid gzip-compressed tar.
   • tar -tf diagnostics_bundle.tar.gz returns exactly one entry:
        summary.log
   • The entry’s content must be byte-for-byte identical to /home/user/diagnostics/summary.log.

The verification harness will:
   a. Assert presence of the directory, file, and archive.
   b. In a temporary location, extract diagnostics_bundle.tar.gz and compare the extracted summary.log against the on-disk version using sha256sum; mismatch is a failure.
   c. Re-compute the expected three diagnostic values (hostname, kernel release, and root filesystem percentage) and perform line-by-line comparison with summary.log.

No other paths, files, or archives should exist under /home/user/diagnostics/ .
